instruction: Demonstrate C++20 ranges and views to build a lazy data processing pipeline that filters, transforms, and flattens nested data structures. Show how views compose without allocating intermediate containers.
output:
<think>
C++20 ranges fundamentally change how we write data processing in C++. The key insight is that views are lazy — they don't allocate memory or perform computation until iterated. Composing views with the pipe operator (|) creates a pipeline description, not an execution.

The pipeline: given nested vectors (vector of vectors), flatten them, filter by a predicate, transform each element, and take the first N results. With traditional C++, this requires multiple loops and intermediate vectors. With ranges, it's one expression.

Composition works because each view adapter returns a new view that wraps the previous one. `std::views::join` flattens nested ranges. `std::views::filter` skips elements not matching a predicate. `std::views::transform` applies a function. `std::views::take` limits the count.

The pipe operator `|` is syntactic sugar: `rng | views::filter(pred)` is equivalent to `views::filter(pred)(rng)`. Multiple pipes chain left-to-right.

Important details: views are non-owning — they reference the original data. The original container must outlive the view. Also, some view combinations are only input_range (single-pass), not forward_range, which affects what operations you can do.

For collecting results, C++23 adds `std::ranges::to<Container>()`. In C++20, we use range-based for or manual construction.

I should show the composition, prove no intermediate allocation, and contrast with the traditional approach.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <string>
#include <ranges>
#include <algorithm>
#include <numeric>

namespace views = std::views;
namespace ranges = std::ranges;

struct Employee {
    std::string name;
    std::string department;
    int salary;
    std::vector<std::string> skills;
};

int main() {
    std::vector<Employee> employees = {
        {"Alice",   "Engineering", 120000, {"C++", "Python", "Rust"}},
        {"Bob",     "Marketing",   80000,  {"Excel", "SQL"}},
        {"Charlie", "Engineering", 140000, {"C++", "Go", "CUDA"}},
        {"Diana",   "Engineering", 110000, {"Python", "ML", "C++"}},
        {"Eve",     "Sales",       90000,  {"CRM", "SQL"}},
        {"Frank",   "Engineering", 130000, {"Rust", "C++", "Wasm"}},
    };

    // Pipeline 1: Filter engineers, transform to salary, compute stats
    // Zero intermediate allocations — all lazy until iterated
    auto engineer_salaries = employees
        | views::filter([](const Employee& e) {
            return e.department == "Engineering";
          })
        | views::transform([](const Employee& e) {
            return e.salary;
          });

    std::cout << "Engineer salaries (lazy view, no allocation):\n";
    for (int salary : engineer_salaries) {
        std::cout << "  $" << salary << "\n";
    }

    // Pipeline 2: Flatten all skills from engineers, deduplicate
    auto engineer_skills = employees
        | views::filter([](const Employee& e) {
            return e.department == "Engineering";
          })
        | views::transform([](const Employee& e) -> const auto& {
            return e.skills;
          })
        | views::join;  // flatten vector<vector<string>> -> range<string>

    std::cout << "\nAll engineer skills (flattened):\n";
    std::vector<std::string> unique_skills;
    for (const auto& skill : engineer_skills) {
        if (ranges::find(unique_skills, skill) == unique_skills.end()) {
            unique_skills.push_back(skill);
        }
    }
    for (const auto& s : unique_skills) {
        std::cout << "  " << s << "\n";
    }

    // Pipeline 3: Enumerate with index, take first 3
    auto top3_engineers = employees
        | views::filter([](const Employee& e) {
            return e.department == "Engineering";
          })
        | views::transform([](const Employee& e) {
            return e.name + " ($" + std::to_string(e.salary) + ")";
          })
        | views::take(3);

    std::cout << "\nFirst 3 engineers:\n";
    for (const auto& desc : top3_engineers) {
        std::cout << "  " << desc << "\n";
    }

    // Pipeline 4: iota + transform for generating data
    auto squares = views::iota(1, 20)
        | views::filter([](int n) { return n % 2 != 0; })
        | views::transform([](int n) { return n * n; })
        | views::take(5);

    std::cout << "\nFirst 5 odd squares:\n";
    for (int sq : squares) {
        std::cout << "  " << sq << "\n";
    }

    // Pipeline 5: reverse view (no copy, just reversed iteration)
    auto reversed_names = employees
        | views::transform([](const Employee& e) -> const std::string& {
            return e.name;
          })
        | views::reverse
        | views::take(3);

    std::cout << "\nLast 3 employees (reversed view):\n";
    for (const auto& name : reversed_names) {
        std::cout << "  " << name << "\n";
    }

    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Build a custom C++20 range adaptor that implements a 'chunk_by' operation — grouping consecutive elements that satisfy a binary predicate into sub-ranges. Make it work with the pipe operator and standard range algorithms.
output:
<think>
C++23 has `std::views::chunk_by` but C++20 doesn't. Building it from scratch teaches the range adaptor machinery.

A range adaptor needs:
1. A view class that satisfies the `std::ranges::view` concept
2. An iterator that knows how to find group boundaries
3. A range adaptor closure object for the pipe operator

The view stores a reference to the underlying range and the predicate. Its iterator finds the end of the current group by scanning forward until the predicate returns false between consecutive elements.

The iterator's value_type is a subrange of the underlying range — each dereference yields a subrange [group_start, group_end). Incrementing advances to the next group.

For the pipe operator: I need a range adaptor closure. In C++20, this is a callable object where `range | adaptor(pred)` is equivalent to `adaptor(pred)(range)`. I'll implement this with a helper struct.

Key implementation details:
- The view must model `std::ranges::view_interface` for begin/end
- The iterator must model `std::forward_iterator` at minimum
- Finding group boundaries: starting from current position, advance while pred(current, next) is true
- Sentinel: we need to handle the end of the underlying range

I'll use `std::ranges::subrange` for the yielded groups, which gives callers a proper range they can iterate over.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <string>
#include <ranges>
#include <algorithm>
#include <functional>
#include <iterator>

namespace custom_views {

template<std::ranges::forward_range V, typename Pred>
    requires std::ranges::view<V> &&
             std::indirect_binary_predicate<Pred,
                 std::ranges::iterator_t<V>, std::ranges::iterator_t<V>>
class chunk_by_view : public std::ranges::view_interface<chunk_by_view<V, Pred>> {
    V base_;
    Pred pred_;

public:
    class iterator {
        using base_iter = std::ranges::iterator_t<V>;
        using base_sent = std::ranges::sentinel_t<V>;

        chunk_by_view* parent_ = nullptr;
        base_iter current_{};
        base_iter next_group_{};

        void find_next_group() {
            next_group_ = current_;
            if (next_group_ == std::ranges::end(parent_->base_)) return;

            auto prev = next_group_;
            ++next_group_;
            while (next_group_ != std::ranges::end(parent_->base_)) {
                if (!parent_->pred_(*prev, *next_group_)) break;
                prev = next_group_;
                ++next_group_;
            }
        }

    public:
        using iterator_category = std::forward_iterator_tag;
        using value_type = std::ranges::subrange<base_iter>;
        using difference_type = std::ptrdiff_t;

        iterator() = default;
        iterator(chunk_by_view* parent, base_iter start)
            : parent_(parent), current_(start) {
            find_next_group();
        }

        value_type operator*() const {
            return {current_, next_group_};
        }

        iterator& operator++() {
            current_ = next_group_;
            find_next_group();
            return *this;
        }

        iterator operator++(int) {
            auto tmp = *this;
            ++*this;
            return tmp;
        }

        bool operator==(const iterator& other) const {
            return current_ == other.current_;
        }

        bool operator==(std::default_sentinel_t) const {
            return current_ == std::ranges::end(parent_->base_);
        }
    };

    chunk_by_view() = default;
    chunk_by_view(V base, Pred pred)
        : base_(std::move(base)), pred_(std::move(pred)) {}

    iterator begin() {
        return iterator(this, std::ranges::begin(base_));
    }

    std::default_sentinel_t end() {
        return std::default_sentinel;
    }
};

// Deduction guide
template<class R, class P>
chunk_by_view(R&&, P) -> chunk_by_view<std::views::all_t<R>, P>;

// Range adaptor closure for pipe operator
template<typename Pred>
struct chunk_by_adaptor {
    Pred pred;

    template<std::ranges::viewable_range R>
    auto operator()(R&& r) const {
        return chunk_by_view(std::views::all(std::forward<R>(r)), pred);
    }

    template<std::ranges::viewable_range R>
    friend auto operator|(R&& r, const chunk_by_adaptor& self) {
        return self(std::forward<R>(r));
    }
};

template<typename Pred>
auto chunk_by(Pred pred) {
    return chunk_by_adaptor<Pred>{std::move(pred)};
}

} // namespace custom_views

int main() {
    // Group consecutive equal elements
    std::vector<int> nums = {1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 3, 1, 1, 3, 3, 3, 3};

    std::cout << "Group equal elements:\n";
    for (auto group : nums | custom_views::chunk_by(std::equal_to{})) {
        std::cout << "  [";
        for (auto it = group.begin(); it != group.end(); ++it) {
            if (it != group.begin()) std::cout << ", ";
            std::cout << *it;
        }
        std::cout << "]\n";
    }

    // Group by non-decreasing runs
    std::vector<int> data = {1, 3, 5, 2, 4, 6, 8, 1, 3};

    std::cout << "\nNon-decreasing runs:\n";
    for (auto group : data | custom_views::chunk_by(std::less_equal{})) {
        std::cout << "  [";
        bool first = true;
        for (int val : group) {
            if (!first) std::cout << ", ";
            std::cout << val;
            first = false;
        }
        std::cout << "]\n";
    }

    // Compose with other views
    std::cout << "\nGroups larger than 2 elements:\n";
    for (auto group : nums | custom_views::chunk_by(std::equal_to{})) {
        if (std::ranges::distance(group) > 2) {
            std::cout << "  [" << *group.begin() << " x"
                      << std::ranges::distance(group) << "]\n";
        }
    }

    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Show how to use C++20 ranges to implement a lazy Sieve of Eratosthenes that generates primes on demand using views::filter, views::iota, and custom view composition. Explain the performance implications of lazy vs eager evaluation.
output:
<think>
The Sieve of Eratosthenes traditionally requires an eager allocation of a boolean array up to N. A lazy version generates primes on demand without knowing the upper bound — perfect for ranges.

However, a truly lazy sieve using nested filters is O(n * primes_found) per element — much worse than the O(n log log n) classic sieve. Each new prime adds a filter layer, and every candidate must pass through ALL filter layers. This is the "unfaithful sieve" — it's trial division in disguise.

For a practical lazy prime generator, I should:
1. Show the naive nested-filter approach (elegant but slow)
2. Show an optimized approach that maintains a set of composite markers (incremental sieve)
3. Compare performance characteristics

The naive approach: start with iota(2), take the first element as prime, filter out its multiples, recurse. In C++20 ranges, this is tricky because views are not easily recursive. I'll use a coroutine-like approach with a generator class.

The optimized incremental sieve: maintain a map from the next composite to the prime that generates it. For each candidate n: if n is in the map, it's composite (advance all its prime factors). If not, it's prime (add n*n to the map). This is O(n log log n) amortized.

I'll implement both and show the lazy evaluation benefits: we never allocate memory proportional to the upper bound, and we can stop at any time.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <ranges>
#include <unordered_map>
#include <cmath>
#include <chrono>

namespace views = std::views;

// Approach 1: Naive lazy sieve using views::filter
// Elegant but O(n * pi(n)) — each candidate tested against all found primes
class NaiveLazySieve {
    std::vector<int> found_primes_;
    int current_ = 2;

public:
    int next() {
        while (true) {
            int candidate = current_++;
            bool is_prime = true;
            int limit = static_cast<int>(std::sqrt(candidate));
            for (int p : found_primes_) {
                if (p > limit) break;
                if (candidate % p == 0) {
                    is_prime = false;
                    break;
                }
            }
            if (is_prime) {
                found_primes_.push_back(candidate);
                return candidate;
            }
        }
    }
};

// Approach 2: Incremental sieve (O'Neill's genuine sieve)
// O(n log log n) amortized, truly lazy, no upper bound needed
class IncrementalSieve {
    // Map: next_composite -> list of primes that generate it
    std::unordered_map<int64_t, std::vector<int64_t>> composites_;
    int64_t candidate_ = 2;

public:
    int64_t next() {
        while (true) {
            int64_t c = candidate_++;
            auto it = composites_.find(c);

            if (it == composites_.end()) {
                // c is prime — mark c*c as next composite from c
                composites_[c * c].push_back(c);
                return c;
            }

            // c is composite — advance each prime's marker
            for (int64_t prime : it->second) {
                composites_[c + prime].push_back(prime);
            }
            composites_.erase(it); // clean up
        }
    }
};

// Wrap as a C++20-compatible view using a simple range
template<typename Sieve>
class SieveView : public std::ranges::view_interface<SieveView<Sieve>> {
    mutable Sieve sieve_;

    struct iterator {
        SieveView* parent;
        int64_t current;
        int count;

        using iterator_category = std::input_iterator_tag;
        using value_type = int64_t;
        using difference_type = std::ptrdiff_t;

        int64_t operator*() const { return current; }
        iterator& operator++() {
            current = parent->sieve_.next();
            ++count;
            return *this;
        }
        iterator operator++(int) { auto t = *this; ++*this; return t; }
        bool operator==(std::default_sentinel_t) const { return false; } // infinite
    };

public:
    auto begin() {
        auto first = sieve_.next();
        return iterator{this, first, 0};
    }
    auto end() { return std::default_sentinel; }
};

int main() {
    // Use incremental sieve as a lazy range
    SieveView<IncrementalSieve> primes;

    // Compose with standard views
    std::cout << "First 20 primes:\n";
    for (auto p : primes | views::take(20)) {
        std::cout << p << " ";
    }
    std::cout << "\n";

    // Primes between 100 and 200
    SieveView<IncrementalSieve> primes2;
    std::cout << "\nPrimes in [100, 200]:\n";
    for (auto p : primes2
            | views::drop_while([](int64_t p) { return p < 100; })
            | views::take_while([](int64_t p) { return p <= 200; })) {
        std::cout << p << " ";
    }
    std::cout << "\n";

    // Performance comparison
    auto bench = [](auto label, auto fn) {
        auto start = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
        fn();
        auto end = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
        auto ms = std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::milliseconds>(end - start);
        std::cout << label << ": " << ms.count() << "ms\n";
    };

    std::cout << "\nBenchmark (first 10000 primes):\n";
    bench("Naive trial division", []() {
        NaiveLazySieve s;
        for (int i = 0; i < 10000; i++) s.next();
    });
    bench("Incremental sieve   ", []() {
        IncrementalSieve s;
        for (int i = 0; i < 10000; i++) s.next();
    });

    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Implement a C++20 range adaptor that performs 'scan' (running accumulation) lazily, similar to std::partial_sum but working with any range and supporting custom binary operations. Show it composing with other views.
output:
<think>
A scan/prefix-sum view takes a binary operation and an optional initial value, yielding running accumulations. For input [1, 2, 3, 4] with addition: [1, 3, 6, 10]. This is like Haskell's scanl1.

The view needs:
- An accumulator that starts with the first element (or an explicit init value)
- Each iteration: read next element, apply binary op with accumulator, yield new accumulator
- Must be lazy — only compute on iteration

The view struct: stores base range, binary operation, optional initial value. The iterator: maintains current accumulator state, reads from base iterator.

Two variants:
1. scan1 (no init): first element passed through, subsequent elements accumulated
2. scan (with init): init is the first yielded value, then accumulate

I'll implement both. The tricky part is handling the first element differently in scan1.

For pipe operator support: same pattern as before — adaptor closure object.

The iterator must satisfy at least input_iterator. Since we maintain state (accumulator), it can't really be bidirectional — going backwards would require recomputing. Forward is possible if we cache the accumulator at each position, but that defeats laziness. I'll make it input_iterator for simplicity.

Composition example: iota(1) | scan(+) | take(10) gives the triangular numbers.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <ranges>
#include <functional>
#include <optional>
#include <string>

namespace custom_views {

template<std::ranges::input_range V, typename BinOp, typename T>
    requires std::ranges::view<V>
class scan_view : public std::ranges::view_interface<scan_view<V, BinOp, T>> {
    V base_;
    BinOp op_;
    std::optional<T> init_;

public:
    class iterator {
        using base_iter = std::ranges::iterator_t<V>;

        scan_view* parent_ = nullptr;
        base_iter current_{};
        T accumulator_{};
        bool started_ = false;

    public:
        using iterator_category = std::input_iterator_tag;
        using value_type = T;
        using difference_type = std::ptrdiff_t;

        iterator() = default;
        iterator(scan_view* parent, base_iter start)
            : parent_(parent), current_(start) {
            if (current_ != std::ranges::end(parent_->base_)) {
                if (parent_->init_) {
                    accumulator_ = parent_->op_(*parent_->init_, *current_);
                } else {
                    accumulator_ = static_cast<T>(*current_);
                }
                started_ = true;
            }
        }

        T operator*() const { return accumulator_; }

        iterator& operator++() {
            ++current_;
            if (current_ != std::ranges::end(parent_->base_)) {
                accumulator_ = parent_->op_(accumulator_, static_cast<T>(*current_));
            }
            return *this;
        }

        void operator++(int) { ++*this; }

        bool operator==(std::default_sentinel_t) const {
            return current_ == std::ranges::end(parent_->base_);
        }
    };

    scan_view() = default;
    scan_view(V base, BinOp op, std::optional<T> init = std::nullopt)
        : base_(std::move(base)), op_(std::move(op)), init_(std::move(init)) {}

    iterator begin() {
        return iterator(this, std::ranges::begin(base_));
    }
    std::default_sentinel_t end() { return std::default_sentinel; }
};

// Adaptor closures
template<typename BinOp, typename T>
struct scan_adaptor {
    BinOp op;
    std::optional<T> init;

    template<std::ranges::viewable_range R>
    auto operator()(R&& r) const {
        return scan_view<std::views::all_t<R>, BinOp, T>(
            std::views::all(std::forward<R>(r)), op, init);
    }

    template<std::ranges::viewable_range R>
    friend auto operator|(R&& r, const scan_adaptor& self) {
        return self(std::forward<R>(r));
    }
};

// Factory: scan without initial value
template<typename T = int, typename BinOp>
auto scan(BinOp op) {
    return scan_adaptor<BinOp, T>{std::move(op), std::nullopt};
}

// Factory: scan with initial value
template<typename T, typename BinOp>
auto scan(BinOp op, T init) {
    return scan_adaptor<BinOp, T>{std::move(op), std::optional<T>(std::move(init))};
}

} // namespace custom_views

int main() {
    // Running sum (triangular numbers)
    std::cout << "Triangular numbers (first 10):\n  ";
    for (auto n : std::views::iota(1)
            | custom_views::scan<int>(std::plus{})
            | std::views::take(10)) {
        std::cout << n << " ";
    }
    std::cout << "\n";

    // Running product (factorials)
    std::cout << "\nFactorials (first 10):\n  ";
    for (auto n : std::views::iota(1)
            | custom_views::scan<int64_t>(std::multiplies{})
            | std::views::take(10)) {
        std::cout << n << " ";
    }
    std::cout << "\n";

    // Running max
    std::vector<int> data = {3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6, 5, 3};
    std::cout << "\nRunning max of [3,1,4,1,5,9,2,6,5,3]:\n  ";
    for (auto n : data
            | custom_views::scan<int>([](int a, int b) { return std::max(a, b); })) {
        std::cout << n << " ";
    }
    std::cout << "\n";

    // String concatenation scan
    std::vector<std::string> words = {"hello", " ", "world", "!"};
    std::cout << "\nString accumulation:\n";
    for (const auto& s : words
            | custom_views::scan<std::string>(std::plus{})) {
        std::cout << "  \"" << s << "\"\n";
    }

    // Compose: running sum, then filter even, then take 5
    std::cout << "\nEven running sums (first 5):\n  ";
    for (auto n : std::views::iota(1)
            | custom_views::scan<int>(std::plus{})
            | std::views::filter([](int n) { return n % 2 == 0; })
            | std::views::take(5)) {
        std::cout << n << " ";
    }
    std::cout << "\n";

    return 0;
}
```
